solution.p: We will factor y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2} where a=1, b=-8, and c=16.

Perfect Square Formula: The formula for perfect square trinomials is (a \pm b)^2=a^2 \pm 2ab + b^2. Note that the polynomial y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2} matches the formula's right side, and we have: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2}\amp =\left(y\right)^2-2\left(y\right)\left(4x\right)+\left(4x\right)^2\newline \amp =\left(y-4x\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-4x\right)\newline \amp =\left(y-4x\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
Mental Method: When we factor a polynomial in the form of x^2+bx+c, x^2+bxy+cy^2, or x^2y^2+bxy+c we can use mental math to factor. We need to find two numbers whose product is c, and whose sum is b.

For y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2}, since the product of -4 and -4 is 16, and the sum of -4 and -4 is -8, the factorization is \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2}\amp =\left(y-4x\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-4x\right)\newline \amp =\left(y-4x\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
AC Method: In this method, we first calculate the product of a and c in ax^2+bx+c. For this problem, we have (1) \cdot (16) = 16.

The number 16 can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways:  egin{array}{lll}1(16)\amp -1(-16)\amp 2(8)\amp -2(-8)
ewline4(4)\amp -4(-4)\amp \end{array} Looking at the corresponding sums, we want a sum of -8, and this sum happens with (-4)+(-4)=-8 So we write  egin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2}\amp =y^{2}-4yx-4yx+16x^{2}
ewline \amp =y\mathopen{}\left(y-4xight)-4x\mathopen{}\left(y-4xight)
ewline \amp =\left(y-4xight)\mathopen{}\left(y-4xight)
ewline \amp =\left(y-4xight)^{2}\end{aligned}Generic Rectangles Method: First, we set up generic rectangles by putting the first term and third term in y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2} into the top left and bottom right rectangles:
The first term's coefficient, 1, can be factored into the product of two numbers (where the first factor is positive) in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(1)\amp \end{array}

The third term's coefficient, 16, can be factored into the product of two numbers in the following ways: \begin{array}{llll}1(16)\amp -1(-16)\amp 2(8)\amp -2(-8)\newline4(4)\amp -4(-4)\amp \end{array}

We put each pair into the corresponding places next to those generic rectangles, and try to match the area of those rectangles with y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2} by guess-and-check. The following generic rectangles show the solution:

Dimensions of those generic rectangles in the diagram give us the solution: \begin{aligned}[t]y^{2}-8yx+16x^{2}\amp =\left(y-4x\right)\mathopen{}\left(y-4x\right)\newline \amp =\left(y-4x\right)^{2}\end{aligned}
